title: Chamaesium novem-jugum
intro: Chamaesium novem-jugum, commonly known as nine-holed wormwood, is a perennial plant species belonging to the Apiaceae family. It has been traditionally used in herbal medicine to treat various health conditions, including digestive issues, fever, and inflammation, due to its anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, and antimicrobial properties. The bioactive constituents of Chamaesium novem-jugum include sesquiterpenes, flavonoids, and essential oils, which are responsible for its medicinal effects. The most common herbal preparations made with this plant include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to support digestive health and relieve symptoms of fever and inflammation.
taxonomy: Chamaesium novem-jugum is classified in the family Apiaceae, which is a large and diverse family of flowering plants commonly known as the carrot or parsley family. This family is further divided into several subfamilies and tribes. Chamaesium novem-jugum is also a member of the tribe Selineae, a group of plants that are typically found in mountainous regions. The species Chamaesium novem-jugum is native to New Zealand and is a type of flowering plant. It is a herbaceous perennial with fern-like foliage.
